title: Docs Viewer Source And Publication Model Refactor Delivery
summary: Implement the role-first source, build, published, and location model before applying its folder, storage, build, and migration consequences.
content_html:
Docs Viewer Source And Publication Model Refactor Delivery

Outcome

Docs Viewer first gains one role-first source, build, published, location, exposure, and ownership model plus provider-independent workflows. The folder cutover, media-type configuration, builder seams, and file moves implement that model rather than being the feature's primary purpose.

Every configured scope then uses one explicit ownership contract: flat canonical Markdown under documents/, build-source media under source media/, nested collections under sub-scopes/, and directly usable media stored once at its registered published root. Repo-backed scopes select repository or R2 backing per media type independently of whether they also have a public projection; external-local scopes keep every source and published media type inside their external scope folder. Library and Analysis images move directly from R2 into their tracked site scope roots, Moments images and ordinary downloadable files remain in R2, and document references stay stable.

The Docs Viewer-owned repository and external-local output workspaces become published/ rather than generated/. Scope configuration becomes the only topology authority; public site/assets/, R2 locations, and logical service routes remain independent.

Benefits And Risks

Benefits:

  • source ownership, document flatness, media identity, and sub-scope boundaries become visible from the filesystem;
  • small directly usable assets become reviewable repository-published artifacts while large or externally originated binaries avoid redundant copies;
  • manual outputs and future Docs Viewer-owned media builders share one published target contract;
  • changing a repo-backed media type between repository and R2 becomes a config-and-file migration rather than a document rewrite;
  • build, import, inventory, and publish orchestration use one role-based workflow while location helpers contain filesystem and R2 differences;
  • external-local scopes gain the same visible structure without acquiring a repository/R2 decision or losing their self-contained lifecycle.

Risks:

  • the cutover touches every source reader, writer, watcher, lifecycle path, builder, import path, and public projection;
  • moving public assets can cause broken document renders unless source, config, built payloads, and publication change together;
  • R2 contents may include unreferenced, stale, or uniquely held bytes, so remote cleanup requires byte-level evidence and an explicit final target list;
  • external-local scopes need the same migration contract without assuming repository paths or silently losing iCloud-backed content.

Delivery Boundary

In scope:

  • the canonical document/build-source workspace and published-media topology;
  • flat documents/, typed build-source media/, namespaced sub-scopes/, and registered published media roots for repo-backed and external-local scopes;
  • a shared role/location contract and repository, external-local, and R2 adapters that keep provider branching out of the main build, import, inventory, and publish workflows;
  • repository and external-local Docs Viewer output roots renamed from generated/ to published/, with configuration replacing literal path assumptions across validation, lifecycle, serving, and browser projection;
  • media-type-level repository/R2 storage configuration for repo-backed scopes, with stable logical media identity and no dependency on public projection status;
  • external-local media kept entirely beneath each external scope's registered media/ root;
  • source loading, mutation, import, lifecycle, watcher, builder, media inventory, serving, publication, and validation changes required by the cutover;
  • migration of current repo and external-local document source;
  • migration of Analysis Tags and the Library HTML media asset into their registered document/sub-scope and published-media roots;
  • exact Docs Viewer R2 inventory, reference comparison, Library/Analysis image migration, and confirmed redundant-object cleanup;
  • focused current-state documentation and removal of superseded paths/contracts without compatibility aliases.

Out of scope:

  • implementing the Mermaid renderer or another new format builder;
  • new video, audio, Processing, or visualization rendering features;
  • redesigning the working Catalogue/Moments R2 media system;
  • automatic lifecycle cleanup for independently owned External Asset Collections;
  • cache fingerprinting, object versioning, purge automation, or a general R2 asset manager;
  • moving ready PDFs, Word files, archives, or other downloads into repository publication merely for structural symmetry.

Documentation Impact

  • Source, Build, Published, And Location Concept contains the complete role/location section to copy intact into the durable owner at closeout.
  • Source Organisation is the primary durable owner for the shipped source, published-media, ownership, and build model.
  • Media And Asset Handling owns media types, storage selection, serving, import placement, and publication behavior.
  • Scope Lifecycle owns creation, rename, delete, and external-local migration consequences.
  • Embedded Detail Documents owns the shipped sub-scope path and payload consequences.
  • Diagrams should point to the resulting Mermaid source/build boundary without absorbing this refactor's implementation history.

Code Authority To Inspect

  • docs-viewer/config/scopes/ for scope topology, browser-safe projection, storage modes, and lifecycle ownership;
  • docs-viewer/build/docs_builder/ and search builders for source discovery, media projection order, token resolution, generated paths, and publication;
  • docs-viewer/services/docs_source_model.py, source mutation/import services, rebuild coordination, and the live watcher for canonical reads/writes and follow-through;
  • scope and sub-scope lifecycle services for create, rename, delete, containment, and external-local path migration;
  • media storage, import, R2 publication, and serving services for the current storage-mode branches, local roots, remote keys, authentication, staging, and byte movement that must move behind the common location boundary;
  • focused Python, route/service, generator, and public-readonly checks that prove those ownership and projection boundaries.

These are responsibility areas, not a permanent module inventory.

Delivery Tracker

Current state: planned and blocking all other unfinished Docs Viewer roadmap work.

Next checkpoint: SSR-1 — produce the write-free source, published-media, document-payload, public, and R2 inventory and freeze the exact cutover mapping before changing paths.

Complete checkpoints in order. Put object lists, hashes, path tables, command transcripts, and migration logs in a temporary sibling working note if they need to survive between sessions; keep this delivery decision- and state-focused.

[ ] SSR-1 — Inventory And Freeze The Cutover

  • [ ] Enumerate every configured repo-backed and external-local scope document/build-source root, document/search payload root, sub-scope root, published media root, and public projection.
  • [ ] Record the current repository/external-local/R2 support matrix for document source, build output, published media, and public snapshots, including every validation or workflow branch that prevents another location.
  • [ ] Enumerate every production, configuration, lifecycle, serving, browser, manifest, test, and documentation dependency on the literal repository or external-local generated/ path; keep logical service routes and public site/assets/ paths classified separately.
  • [ ] List actual Docs Viewer R2 objects under each scope prefix and compare them with canonical document references, size/hash evidence, and any identical repository or external originals.
  • [ ] Classify every existing media object by scope, type, semantic role, producer/origin, current authority, intended backing, target path, and cleanup disposition; external-local targets must remain beneath their external scope folders.
  • [ ] Freeze the exact config schema, source/published/public path mapping, logical URL behavior, migration order, and failure/recovery boundary before the first move.

Checkpoint evidence: one write-free plan accounts for every current canonical document, sub-scope, owned media file, referenced remote object, target path, and proposed deletion without treating a reference list as the bucket inventory.

[ ] SSR-2 — Establish The Role And Location Model

  • [ ] Replace storage-mode-shaped configuration with explicit artifact role configuration: canonical source location where one exists, optional build/producer, exactly one published location per media type, and public exposure as a separate concern.
  • [ ] Introduce one role/location boundary for repository, external-local, and R2 backing, covering confined identity resolution, list/read/write/replace/delete/stat, byte verification, served-reference resolution, authentication, temporary local staging, and capability reporting.
  • [ ] Make build, import, inventory, and publish orchestration depend only on logical artifact identity, semantic role, and required capabilities; remove provider-specific branches from those main workflows.
  • [ ] Validate role/location combinations by required adapter capability and explicit scope policy rather than by historical storage-mode assumptions.
  • [ ] Express every current scope and media mapping through the new model without moving bytes, changing references, or altering current outputs.
  • [ ] Remove literal Docs Viewer generated/ root construction and validation from production code so configured locations are authoritative for builders, readers, lifecycle, serving, manifests, and generated browser configuration.
  • [ ] Change the configured repository and external-local output roots to published/, move the existing artifacts, and keep public site/assets/, R2 locations, and logical service routes unchanged.

Checkpoint evidence: the current repository/external-local/R2 support matrix is reproduced through one common workflow boundary, adapter-level checks exercise each provider, unsupported combinations report missing capabilities or policy constraints precisely, and current builds/imports/publication remain byte- and route-equivalent. After config changes and artifact moves, no production code requires the old physical generated/ roots and all Docs Viewer local output resolves from published/.

[ ] SSR-3 — Cut Over Documents And Sub-Scopes

  • [ ] Make the configured source path an ownership root and resolve normal canonical Markdown only from its flat documents/ child.
  • [ ] Resolve configured sub-scopes beneath sub-scopes/<id>/, with their Markdown flat beneath documents/, while keeping them excluded from the parent tree and search.
  • [ ] Update builders, search, source reads/writes, imports, capabilities, watcher reconciliation, lifecycle planning, external-local rename/delete, and path validation to the new contract without fallback reads or aliases.
  • [ ] Move every repo-backed source document and configured sub-scope, then apply the same previewed path-safe migration to available external-local scopes while preserving bytes, ids, metadata, and hierarchy.

Checkpoint evidence: every configured scope and sub-scope reads, writes, rebuilds, watches, renames, and deletes through only the new paths and common location boundary; source bytes and document identities are unchanged by the move.

[ ] SSR-4 — Establish Published Media Ownership

  • [ ] Replace behavioral structural naming with typed media boundaries, including img, files, html, and mermaid, while allowing later types only through explicit validation, build, and handling contracts.
  • [ ] Inventory media across registered repository-published roots, R2 prefixes, and external-local scope folders, then associate it with document references without requiring every owned asset to be referenced.
  • [ ] Store already usable images, files, HTML, SVG, video, and manual renders only at their configured published root; imports and manual placement write there directly rather than creating source/published duplicates.
  • [ ] Make full builds invoke type-specific producers directly into the same published root before rendering documents, reject competing producers, and never write reproducible outputs back into source.
  • [ ] Move coincidence-salience-demo.html to site/assets/data/docs/scopes/library/media/html/, replace interactive-as-structure path/token assumptions with an HTML-media sandbox contract, and keep that registered published artifact as its only copy.

Checkpoint evidence: the Library HTML asset has one registered tracked published location, its document renders through the sandboxed HTML handler, no redundant source/published copy exists, and one provider-independent workflow can reach repository, external-local, or R2 published locations. The same target seam can accept a later Mermaid producer without implementing one now.

[ ] SSR-5 — Apply The Repository And R2 Policy

  • [ ] Apply the validated per-type role/location mappings for repo-backed scopes whose logical scope/type/filename identity does not depend on the backing provider or public projection status.
  • [ ] Configure Library and Analysis img as repository-backed; move and verify their currently referenced R2 images directly under site/assets/data/docs/scopes/<scope>/media/img/ without adding source or secondary working copies.
  • [ ] Keep Moments img on its established R2 path, keep ordinary files on R2, and keep directly usable HTML/SVG plus build-source Mermaid in their appropriate single repository roots.
  • [ ] Store directly usable media for a repo-backed scope without a public projection once beneath its configured published scope root; a current physical path named generated does not change the semantic role, and public projection does not decide the repository/R2 policy.
  • [ ] Keep external-local storage outside the repo/R2 policy: every external-local media type resolves beneath its own external scope folder and follows that folder through create, rename, and delete.
  • [ ] Prove public and local document references still resolve, then remove only the exact redundant Library/Analysis R2 objects approved from the SSR-1 inventory; retain unexplained, unique, or externally authoritative objects.

Checkpoint evidence: each repo-backed media type has exactly one authoritative published root selected independently of public projection, every external-local media type remains inside its external scope folder, logical references are unchanged, all migrated bytes match, Moments remains unchanged, and no R2 object is deleted without inventory-backed attribution and a working replacement.

[ ] SSR-6 — Verify, Document, And Close

  • [ ] Prove document/source loading, flatness rejection, sub-scope exclusion, source mutation, import, watcher, lifecycle, external-local, media serving, build order, built output, and public publication at the lowest stable layers.
  • [ ] Prove missing media, conflicting producers, unsupported adapter capabilities, policy-invalid role/location combinations, stale migration plans, path escape, R2 comparison failure, and partial publication failures are contained and clearly reported.
  • [ ] Run the narrowest relevant Docs Viewer checks, git diff --check, and the focused private-path/secret scan; keep detailed transcripts outside this delivery.
  • [ ] Copy the complete Source, Build, Published, Location, And Exposure section from the concept into Source Organisation, update the focused durable documentation owners, update roadmap status only when the complete cutover works, and present the feature-document closeout list for explicit review.

Checkpoint evidence: all Done When conditions pass, current documentation routes to the new authority, no active old source/media path remains, and any remote cleanup is evidenced separately from source migration.

Done When

  • every scope visibly separates flat document source, typed build-source media, namespaced sub-scopes, and registered published media;
  • repository and external-local Docs Viewer output workspaces use published/; public site/assets/, R2 locations, and logical service routes retain their independent contracts;
  • configuration is the sole topology authority, with no production validator, lifecycle path, server, manifest, browser projection, reader, or builder assuming a literal old generated root;
  • every supported reader, writer, import, watcher, lifecycle operation, and builder uses only the new source paths;
  • document identity, hierarchy, body bytes, logical media references, and public/local route meaning survive the cutover;
  • HTML is scope-owned published media whose sandbox behavior does not determine its folder;
  • already usable media is stored only at its authoritative published root, while future format builders write directly to that same target without mutating canonical source;
  • media storage is selected per type and can change through config plus byte movement without document rewrites;
  • build, import, inventory, and publish orchestration contains no repository/external-local/R2 workflow forks; common location helpers own provider-specific authentication, resolution, staging, I/O, and capability checks;
  • external-local scopes use the same document/media/sub-scope structure while retaining every media type beneath their external scope folders;
  • Library and Analysis images exist only in their tracked public scope media roots, Moments images remain on their established R2 path, and ordinary downloadable files remain R2-backed;
  • actual R2 objects, document references, local duplicates, migrations, and retained/deleted objects are fully reconciled;
  • every published media object is either placed directly or reproducible from registered build source, with no duplicate or ambiguous competing authority;
  • focused source, lifecycle, media, builder, publication, and public-isolation evidence passes;
  • the complete concept section has been promoted into Source Organisation, and the focused Media And Asset Handling, Scope Lifecycle, and Embedded Detail Documents references describe the shipped consequences without competing authority.
